backend/debug.py.short_description: Interactive CLI debug script to run the lead_agent with breakpoints and manual input.
backend/debug.py.description:
This script exists as a developer convenience to run the lead_agent interactively from a terminal (or VS Code) with the ability to set breakpoints and step through agent behavior. It loads environment variables via dotenv, attempts to initialize MCP tools if available, constructs a lead agent using the project's make_lead_agent factory, and then enters a synchronous input loop that converts typed lines into HumanMessage objects and invokes the agent asynchronously (agent.ainvoke). The main use-case is ad-hoc/manual debugging of the agent flow, middleware, tools and subagents while developing — not automated test runs.

Design choices reflect that it's a lightweight entry point rather than a reusable library component: it configures logging at module startup, tolerantly attempts to initialize optional MCP tools (printing a warning on failure), and creates a simple default config dict passed to make_lead_agent. The script explicitly documents and requires being run via `uv run` from backend/ so the workspace package layout resolves correctly (PYTHONPATH=.). The input loop handles Control-C and general exceptions by printing tracebacks and continues to be interactive until the user types 'quit' or 'exit'.

key_constructs.name: main
key_constructs.purpose: Async entrypoint that initializes optional MCP tools, creates the lead agent, and runs an interactive REPL loop to send HumanMessage inputs to the agent.
key_constructs.reasoning: An async function is required because agent.ainvoke is an async call. The function bundles one-off initialization (MCP tools) and the interactive loop making it straightforward to run under asyncio.run() for debugging.

key_behaviors: initializes optional MCP tools at startup
creates a lead agent with a minimal default config
accepts user input from stdin and wraps it as HumanMessage
invokes agent.ainvoke asynchronously and prints agent responses
handles KeyboardInterrupt and prints exception tracebacks

pitfalls.mistake: Running the script directly with `python backend/debug.py` from the repository root or wrong cwd.
pitfalls.consequence: Imports for in-repo packages (deerflow-harness, app packages) will fail because the uv workspace paths are not set; ModuleNotFoundError or incorrect package resolution may occur.
pitfalls.prevention: Run from backend/ with the recommended command: `cd backend && PYTHONPATH=. uv run python debug.py` (this is documented in the file header).

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or suppressing the try/except around initialize_mcp_tools or assuming it always succeeds.
pitfalls.consequence: If MCP initialization fails, an unhandled exception could abort the debug session and obscure other debugging concerns.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing tolerant behavior — keep initialization inside a try/except and surface a readable warning rather than crashing.

pitfalls.mistake: Calling asyncio.run(main()) from an existing event loop (e.g., embedding this script into another async environment) without adapting it.
pitfalls.consequence: A RuntimeError will be raised about an already running event loop, making the script fail to start when embedded into other async frameworks or tests.
pitfalls.prevention: When integrating, use appropriate loop-aware patterns (e.g., await main() directly or use asyncio.get_event_loop().create_task) rather than asyncio.run.

key_sections: MCP init: the try/except block that calls initialize_mcp_tools — optional third-party tool initialization
Agent config: the `config` dict in main which defines thread_id, thinking_enabled, is_plan_mode and optional model_name
Agent creation and invocation: make_lead_agent(config) and the REPL loop that sends HumanMessage inputs and calls agent.ainvoke
skip_unless_needed: logging.basicConfig setup (boilerplate logging configuration)
module docstring and usage instructions (helpful but not code-critical)

tests.example_command: Run interactively: cd backend && PYTHONPATH=. uv run python debug.py

related_files.relationship: Creates the lead Agent instance used by the debug REPL; main calls make_lead_agent to get an agent object.
related_files.reason_to_check: If make_lead_agent signature or config schema changes, update how config is constructed/passed in this script.

related_files.relationship: Optional initialization invoked at startup; failure is tolerated here but behavior affects available tools during debugging.
related_files.reason_to_check: When MCP initialization API changes or becomes required, this script's startup semantics may need to change.

related_files.relationship: HumanMessage instances are constructed from user input and passed into the agent state.
related_files.reason_to_check: If the message schema changes, adapt the way messages are constructed and how state is formed.

data_constants_to_check: configurable keys used in config dict (thread_id, thinking_enabled, is_plan_mode, model_name)

backend/app/gateway/routers/memory.py.short_description: FastAPI router exposing HTTP endpoints to view and manage the global memory snapshot and configuration.
backend/app/gateway/routers/memory.py.description:
This module implements the Gateway memory HTTP API surface for reading, importing/exporting, and CRUD operations on memory 'facts' and for retrieving memory configuration and status. It exists to provide an administrative and programmatic interface to the global memory subsystem (the in-memory cache + persisted storage) so external callers (UI, CLI, automation) can manipulate memory independent of agents. The router is mounted under the /api prefix and uses Pydantic models to define request and response schemas with consistent camelCase field names expected by clients.

Design-wise the module separates schema definitions (Pydantic models: ContextSection, UserContext, HistoryContext, Fact, MemoryResponse, MemoryConfigResponse, MemoryStatusResponse, FactCreateRequest, FactPatchRequest) from route implementations. The route handlers are thin adapters that call into the memory updater API (deerflow.agents.memory.updater functions) and translate errors into appropriate HTTP exceptions. There is a small helper _map_memory_fact_value_error() to normalize ValueError cases (notably the 'confidence' validation path) to a stable 400 response message that tests rely on. Many endpoints use response_model_exclude_none=True so None fields are not emitted to clients.

The module relies on the memory subsystem API (get_memory_data, reload_memory_data, clear_memory_data, create_memory_fact, delete_memory_fact, update_memory_fact, import_memory_data) and on a memory configuration accessor (get_memory_config). It must preserve exact field names (including camelCase like sourceError) and error message text/status codes because tests (and likely clients) assert those exact values. The endpoints covered include retrieval (GET /api/memory), reload (POST /api/memory/reload), clear (DELETE /api/memory), create fact (POST /api/memory/facts), delete fact (DELETE /api/memory/facts/{fact_id}), patch fact (PATCH /api/memory/facts/{fact_id}), export (GET /api/memory/export), import (POST /api/memory/import), get config (GET /api/memory/config), and combined status (GET /api/memory/status).

key_constructs.name: ContextSection
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic model for a single context section containing summary and updatedAt fields.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a minimal typed container for user/history context sections so route responses conform to a stable schema expected by clients.

key_constructs.name: UserContext
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic model grouping user-specific context sections (workContext, personalContext, topOfMind).
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates user context areas used in MemoryResponse; keeps response schema explicit for clients and tests.

key_constructs.name: HistoryContext
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic model grouping history-specific context sections.
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines the history structure in the memory snapshot returned to callers.

key_constructs.name: Fact
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic model representing a single memory fact (id, content, category, confidence, timestamps, source, sourceError).
key_constructs.reasoning: Central domain object handled by create/update/delete endpoints; fields and exact JSON keys (including sourceError) are relied upon by tests and UI.

key_constructs.name: MemoryResponse
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level Pydantic response schema for returning a full memory snapshot (version, lastUpdated, user, history, facts).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used as response_model on many endpoints; ensures JSON payload shape and that optional None fields can be excluded consistently.

key_constructs.name: _map_memory_fact_value_error
key_constructs.purpose: Translate ValueError raised by memory updater into a stable HTTPException with an explicit 400 detail message.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates mapping logic for ValueErrors (distinguishes 'confidence' errors) so endpoints can raise consistent client-facing messages; tests assert the specific message.

key_constructs.name: FactCreateRequest
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic request model for creating a new memory fact (content required, category and confidence optional with defaults and validation).
key_constructs.reasoning: Validates incoming POST payloads for create; enforces non-empty content and confidence range.

key_constructs.name: FactPatchRequest
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic request model for PATCH updates to a fact that allows omitted fields to be preserved (fields optional).
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures PATCH semantics (partial updates) are validated (confidence bounds) while allowing None to indicate omission.

key_constructs.name: get_memory
key_constructs.purpose: GET /api/memory - returns the current in-memory memory snapshot via get_memory_data().
key_constructs.reasoning: Primary read endpoint used by UI and tests; returns MemoryResponse created from the raw memory data dict.

key_constructs.name: reload_memory
key_constructs.purpose: POST /api/memory/reload - forces reload of persisted memory file into in-memory cache via reload_memory_data().
key_constructs.reasoning: Administrative endpoint to refresh the in-process cache if storage changed externally.

key_constructs.name: clear_memory
key_constructs.purpose: DELETE /api/memory - clears persisted memory via clear_memory_data() and returns the new empty snapshot.
key_constructs.reasoning: Administrative operation with OSError handling to signal storage-level failures as HTTP 500.

key_constructs.name: create_memory_fact_endpoint
key_constructs.purpose: POST /api/memory/facts - create a new memory fact through create_memory_fact() and return updated snapshot.
key_constructs.reasoning: Validates request via FactCreateRequest and maps ValueError/OSError into HTTP errors; tests patch create_memory_fact to check behavior.

key_constructs.name: delete_memory_fact_endpoint
key_constructs.purpose: DELETE /api/memory/facts/{fact_id} - delete a fact by id via delete_memory_fact().
key_constructs.reasoning: Maps KeyError -> 404 and OSError -> 500; tests assert the 404 message for missing facts.

key_constructs.name: update_memory_fact_endpoint
key_constructs.purpose: PATCH /api/memory/facts/{fact_id} - partial update via update_memory_fact(), preserving omitted fields.
key_constructs.reasoning: Accepts FactPatchRequest with optional fields; maps ValueError (confidence) -> formatted 400 via helper; tests assert both call args and error mapping.

key_constructs.name: export_memory
key_constructs.purpose: GET /api/memory/export - export the current memory snapshot by calling get_memory_data().
key_constructs.reasoning: Added explicitly for backup/portability; tests verify exported JSON preserves extra fields like sourceError.

key_constructs.name: import_memory
key_constructs.purpose: POST /api/memory/import - import and overwrite memory from a JSON payload by calling import_memory_data().
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses request.model_dump() to obtain dict for import_memory_data(); tests patch import_memory_data to confirm round-trip and preservation of sourceError.

key_constructs.name: get_memory_config_endpoint
key_constructs.purpose: GET /api/memory/config - return memory configuration from get_memory_config().
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposes configuration parameters as MemoryConfigResponse for clients and admin UIs.

key_constructs.name: get_memory_status
key_constructs.purpose: GET /api/memory/status - combine config and data into a single MemoryStatusResponse.
key_constructs.reasoning: Convenience endpoint to fetch both config and snapshot atomically for status pages or diagnostics.

key_behaviors: returns current memory snapshot
imports and exports full memory JSON payloads
creates, updates, and deletes memory facts via updater layer
exposes memory configuration and combined status

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the exact HTTP error messages or status codes produced (e.g., the 'Invalid confidence value...' message or 404 formatting).
pitfalls.consequence: Unit tests will fail because they assert specific status codes and detail strings; clients may rely on these messages.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve error text and status codes when modifying exception handling; if changes are required, update tests and dependent clients in lockstep.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering Pydantic field names or the camelCase JSON keys (e.g., renaming sourceError to source_error).
pitfalls.consequence: Clients and tests expect exact key names; changing them will break serialization and assertions.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the declared model field names unchanged or ensure JSON aliasing is correctly provided and tests updated.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the module import path (app.gateway.routers.memory) or moving the router to a different module without updating tests.
pitfalls.consequence: The tests patch functions using the module path and will fail to patch/mismatch imports, causing test failures.
pitfalls.prevention: If refactoring, update tests' patch targets and any external imports that reference this router path.

reading_guide.start_here: get_memory (the basic read endpoint) or MemoryResponse (schema) to understand the JSON shape
key_sections: MemoryResponse: defines the JSON structure returned by most endpoints
_map_memory_fact_value_error: contains the ValueError -> HTTPException mapping used by multiple endpoints
create_memory_fact_endpoint/update_memory_fact_endpoint/delete_memory_fact_endpoint: show how updater functions are invoked and how errors are translated
skip_unless_needed: Pydantic model docstrings and Field descriptions (boilerplate schema metadata)

relevant_snippets.description: test_export_memory_route_returns_current_memory: patches get_memory_data and asserts /api/memory/export returns the facts payload.
test_import_memory_route_returns_imported_memory: patches import_memory_data and asserts POST /api/memory/import echoes facts back.
test_update_memory_fact_route_returns_specific_error_for_invalid_confidence: patches update_memory_fact to raise ValueError('confidence') and asserts the 400 and exact error text.
test_update_memory_fact_route_preserves_omitted_fields: asserts update_memory_fact was called with None for omitted fields.

related_files.relationship: Back-end implementation that performs get/create/update/delete/import/export of memory snapshots and facts; this router calls those functions directly.
related_files.reason_to_check: If updating signature/behavior or exception types/messages in the updater functions, the router must adapt mappings and tests must be updated.

related_files.relationship: Provides get_memory_config() used by the config/status endpoints.
related_files.reason_to_check: If config model fields change (names, types), the MemoryConfigResponse mapping in this router must be reviewed.

data_constants_to_check: memory config fields returned by deerflow.config.memory_config.get_memory_config() (enabled, storage_path, debounce_seconds, max_facts, fact_confidence_threshold, injection_enabled, max_injection_tokens)

insights.title: Add /memory/export and /memory/import routes
insights.problem: No HTTP endpoints existed to export/import full memory snapshots for backup/restore.
insights.root_cause: Feature missing in Gateway router.
insights.solution: Added GET /memory/export and POST /memory/import endpoints that call get_memory_data and import_memory_data respectively and return MemoryResponse; included response_model_exclude_none in router definitions elsewhere to avoid None fields in responses.
insights.lesson_learned: Provide administrative endpoints for data portability and ensure response models handle optional fields.

backend/tests/test_memory_router.py.short_description: Unit tests for the memory Gateway router verifying import/export and CRUD behaviors and error mappings.
backend/tests/test_memory_router.py.description:
This test module exercises the memory router endpoints by mounting the FastAPI router into a fresh FastAPI app per test and using TestClient to exercise HTTP routes. The tests use unittest.mock.patch to stub out the memory updater and config functions imported inside the router module. This isolates the router layer from the storage/backend implementation and focuses on HTTP behavior, response serialization (including camelCase fields like sourceError), and error translation (notably ValueError('confidence') -> HTTP 400 with a specific message and KeyError -> HTTP 404 with specific detail text).

Tests are comprehensive for the HTTP surface implemented in the router: export/import roundtrips (including preservation of extra fields like sourceError), clearing memory, creating facts, deleting facts (both success and KeyError->404), updating facts (success, omitted-field preservation by asserting the update function receives None for omitted fields, KeyError->404), and the ValueError confidence mapping to a 400 response. The tests directly patch functions using the import path app.gateway.routers.memory.<function>, so any refactor that changes the module path or function names must update tests accordingly.

key_constructs.name: _sample_memory
key_constructs.purpose: Helper to produce a sample memory snapshot dict with optional facts for use in test assertions.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures tests reuse a consistent example payload shape that matches MemoryResponse schema and includes camelCase keys used in assertions.

key_constructs.name: test_export_memory_route_returns_current_memory
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies GET /api/memory/export returns get_memory_data() output.
key_constructs.reasoning: Patches get_memory_data to return a sample and asserts JSON facts match.

key_constructs.name: test_import_memory_route_returns_imported_memory
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies POST /api/memory/import calls import_memory_data and returns its result.
key_constructs.reasoning: Patches import_memory_data and posts JSON; asserts returned facts equal the patched payload.

key_constructs.name: test_export_memory_route_preserves_source_error
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts export preserves extra fact fields like sourceError.
key_constructs.reasoning: Important for round-trip fidelity; ensures router does not drop unexpected but allowed fields.

key_constructs.name: test_clear_memory_route_returns_cleared_memory
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies DELETE /api/memory returns an empty snapshot when clear_memory_data is patched accordingly.
key_constructs.reasoning: Checks basic admin clear operation and response shape.

key_constructs.name: test_create_memory_fact_route_returns_updated_memory
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies create fact route uses create_memory_fact and returns updated memory.
key_constructs.reasoning: Patches create_memory_fact and asserts response JSON.

key_constructs.name: test_delete_memory_fact_route_returns_404_for_missing_fact
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies router maps KeyError from delete_memory_fact to HTTP 404 with a specific detail message.
key_constructs.reasoning: Test asserts exact detail string that the router composes using the fact id.

key_constructs.name: test_update_memory_fact_route_preserves_omitted_fields
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies update endpoint passes None for omitted PATCH fields to update_memory_fact.
key_constructs.reasoning: Asserts update_memory_fact called with content and None for category/confidence when omitted.

key_constructs.name: test_update_memory_fact_route_returns_specific_error_for_invalid_confidence
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies ValueError('confidence') from update_memory_fact results in HTTP 400 and exact detail string.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures validation error mapping remains stable for clients and tests.

key_behaviors: mounts the router into a TestClient app and issues HTTP requests
patches router-internal functions to isolate behavior
asserts JSON payloads and HTTP status codes including exact error details

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the router import path or the names of functions being patched in tests without updating the patch target strings.
pitfalls.consequence: Mocks will not apply and tests will fail or attempt real I/O calls.
pitfalls.prevention: When refactoring, update tests' patch targets to the new import locations.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering exact error detail text or status codes in the router without updating tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests that assert exact strings will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep tests and router error strings synchronized when changing behavior.

reading_guide.start_here: _sample_memory (to understand test payload shape), then the tests that assert behavior you plan to change
key_sections: test_update_memory_fact_route_preserves_omitted_fields: demonstrates expected call signature when PATCH fields are omitted
test_update_memory_fact_route_returns_specific_error_for_invalid_confidence: shows ValueError mapping verification
skip_unless_needed: repetitive test setups that mount the router into a TestClient (boilerplate)

related_files.relationship: The module under test; tests patch functions that are imported into this router.
related_files.reason_to_check: If the router's exported endpoints or error messages change, update tests accordingly.

insights.title: Tests for memory import/export routes preserving sourceError
insights.problem: Ensure import/export endpoints preserve additional fields (e.g., sourceError on facts).
insights.root_cause: Edge-case fields may be dropped if not handled correctly by router or storage.
insights.solution: Added tests verifying export and import round-tripping and sourceError preservation.
insights.lesson_learned: Round-trip tests validate that extra fields in exported payloads are preserved through import/export flows.

backend/app/channels/service.py.short_description: Service that instantiates, starts, stops and reports status for configured IM channels.
backend/app/channels/service.py.description:
This module centralizes lifecycle management for IM channels used by the application. It reads per-channel configuration (typically from the app config under the 'channels' key), resolves backend service URLs (LangGraph & Gateway) using a precedence of explicit config -> environment variables -> hardcoded defaults, instantiates channel implementations lazily via import paths, and orchestrates starting/stopping of ChannelManager plus individual Channel instances. The class ChannelService is designed to own the MessageBus and ChannelStore objects and provide a single place to manage the set of active channels and their session configuration.

Design choices visible in the code: dynamic plugin-style channel loading via a registry mapping channel name -> import path and using a reflection helper resolve_class for imports; explicit URL resolution function _resolve_service_url that pops config values when present and falls back to DEER_FLOW_CHANNELS_LANGGRAPH_URL / DEER_FLOW_CHANNELS_GATEWAY_URL environment variables; a singleton pattern at module level offering get/start/stop helpers to create a single global ChannelService. ChannelService also handles robust error logging around channel import/instantiation/start/stop to keep the manager stable when a single channel fails. The module is defensive about config shapes (it checks for dicts) and exposes status via get_status(), which reports known registry entries and whether each is enabled and running.

key_constructs.name: _CHANNEL_REGISTRY
key_constructs.purpose: Mapping from canonical channel name to a Python import path string used for lazy loading channel classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Agents editing channel support must preserve keys and import paths. This registry is the authoritative list of channel types the service can instantiate.

key_constructs.name: _CHANNELS_LANGGRAPH_URL_ENV
key_constructs.purpose: Environment variable name used as a fallback to resolve LangGraph URL when not provided in config.
key_constructs.reasoning: Environment variable name is relied upon by tests and Docker deployments; keep this exact name if other components or tests set it.

key_constructs.name: _CHANNELS_GATEWAY_URL_ENV
key_constructs.purpose: Environment variable name used as a fallback to resolve Gateway URL when not provided in config.
key_constructs.reasoning: Same as above — used by URL resolution logic and in containerized setups.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_service_url
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve a backend service URL by checking config key, then environment variable, then default value.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function encapsulates precedence rules that tests expect (config override > env var > default). It mutates the passed config dict by popping the config_key when present; callers rely on that mutation.

key_constructs.name: ChannelService
key_constructs.purpose: Core lifecycle manager for all configured channels: builds ChannelManager, holds MessageBus/ChannelStore, instantiates channels, and starts/stops them.
key_constructs.reasoning: Most edits to channel behaviour, session injection, manager wiring, or configuration handling will occur here. The class both reads and mutates the channel configuration dict during init and manages runtime state in _channels. It also relies on ChannelManager, ChannelStore, MessageBus, and dynamic channel classes that must match the Channel base API.

key_constructs.name: from_app_config
key_constructs.purpose: Classmethod to construct ChannelService from the global application config (get_app_config()).
key_constructs.reasoning: When application startup code wants a ChannelService pre-populated from app config, this is used. It reads extra fields on the AppConfig under 'channels' (AppConfig allows extra fields), so ensure AppConfig shape/extra semantics are respected.

key_constructs.name: start
key_constructs.purpose: Start the ChannelManager and all configured/enabled channels by calling each channel's start() coroutine.
key_constructs.reasoning: Start must be awaited by callers. It inspects the internal _config mapping and respects per-channel 'enabled' flags, not starting non-dict entries. It sets _running after starting everything and logs started channel names.

key_constructs.name: stop
key_constructs.purpose: Stop all running channels and the ChannelManager, clearing internal _channels and resetting _running flag.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function awaits channel.stop() for each active channel but wraps in try/except so failures in stopping one channel do not abort stopping others. It clears _channels and stops manager; callers expect this to release resources and set global singleton to None via stop_channel_service().

key_constructs.name: restart_channel
key_constructs.purpose: Stop and re-create a single named channel using the stored configuration.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is used for dynamic channel management (hot reload). It relies on _start_channel to re-instantiate and will log warnings if channel config is missing or invalid.

key_constructs.name: _start_channel
key_constructs.purpose: Instantiate a channel class via the registry, call its start(), and record it in _channels.
key_constructs.reasoning: Performs dynamic import using deerflow.reflection.resolve_class with base_class=None. It expects channel_cls to have a constructor that accepts bus and config and an async start() method. Errors are logged and return False on failure.

key_constructs.name: get_status
key_constructs.purpose: Return a mapping describing whether each registered channel is enabled (per config) and currently running (in _channels).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by health/status endpoints or admin UI to present which channels are enabled vs actually running. It iterates registry names (so adding a new channel type affects status reporting).

key_constructs.name: get_channel
key_constructs.purpose: Lookup and return a running Channel instance by name, or None if not running.
key_constructs.reasoning: External code that needs direct access to a channel (e.g., to send messages) uses this accessor. The returned object must implement the Channel interface (app.channels.base.Channel).

callers.context: channel = service.get_channel(msg.channel_name) if service else None

key_constructs.name: _channel_service
key_constructs.purpose: Module-level singleton reference to the global ChannelService instance.
key_constructs.reasoning: Module-level state used by application lifecycle. Edits must respect this global so start/stop helper functions behave as intended. No locking; concurrency must be considered by callers.

key_constructs.name: get_channel_service
key_constructs.purpose: Return the module-level ChannelService singleton if created, else None.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by code that wants to interact with channels without starting them.

key_constructs.name: start_channel_service
key_constructs.purpose: Create ChannelService.from_app_config(), start it, and save it in the module-level singleton.
key_constructs.reasoning: Application startup path calls this. It checks and reuses existing instance if present.

key_constructs.name: stop_channel_service
key_constructs.purpose: Stop the global ChannelService singleton and set the module-level reference to None.
key_constructs.reasoning: Called during shutdown to cleanly stop channels; ensures subsequent start_channel_service() will recreate instance.

key_behaviors: resolve backend service URLs from config/env/default
instantiate channel implementations from registry import paths
start and stop ChannelManager and channel instances
report enabled vs running status for known channel types
provide a global singleton ChannelService for app lifecycle

pitfalls.mistake: Treat the channels_config dict passed into ChannelService.__init__ as immutable.
pitfalls.consequence: _resolve_service_url and __init__ intentionally pop keys from the config dict; changes that assume config is unchanged will be incorrect (some keys get removed).
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that config is copied at start (config = dict(channels_config or {})) and that subsequent pops mutate the local config - don't rely on original dict after construction.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing environment variable names or default names without updating tests and container compose.
pitfalls.consequence: Containerized deployments and tests that depend on DEER_FLOW_CHANNELS_LANGGRAPH_URL / DEER_FLOW_CHANNELS_GATEWAY_URL will break and fall back to wrong defaults.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep environment variable constants stable or update all references (tests, Docker compose, CI) atomically.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming start_channel_service is concurrency-safe.
pitfalls.consequence: start_channel_service is not protected by locks; concurrent callers could race creating multiple instances or starting channels twice.
pitfalls.prevention: Call start/stop lifecycle functions from a single orchestrated startup/shutdown path; if adding concurrency, introduce appropriate synchronization.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying _CHANNEL_REGISTRY entries without ensuring channel classes match Channel base API.
pitfalls.consequence: resolve_class may succeed but instantiation/start may fail at runtime because channel implementations must accept bus and config and expose start/stop/is_running.
pitfalls.prevention: When adding new registry entries, ensure the target class implements the Channel interface and update related tests.

key_sections: ChannelService.__init__: builds MessageBus, ChannelStore and ChannelManager; resolves langgraph/gateway URLs and session maps
ChannelService.start: how channels are iterated, enabled flags checked, and _start_channel invoked
ChannelService._start_channel: dynamic import/instantiation and error handling
module-level singleton helpers: start_channel_service / stop_channel_service
skip_unless_needed: the simple get_status and get_channel accessor methods (straightforward)
_CHANNEL_REGISTRY constant reading

relevant_snippets.description: Unit tests that assert _resolve_service_url precedence: env fallback when config missing, and config overrides env when present.

related_files.relationship: ChannelService creates and starts ChannelManager; ChannelManager defaults (DEFAULT_GATEWAY_URL / DEFAULT_LANGGRAPH_URL) are used when env/config keys are absent.
related_files.reason_to_check: If changing URL resolution logic, check defaults and signatures in ChannelManager and tests that assert URL usage.

related_files.relationship: Defines Channel interface (methods/attributes like start/stop/is_running) that channel implementations must implement.
related_files.reason_to_check: When altering how ChannelService constructs or calls channel instances, ensure compatibility with Channel base API.

related_files.relationship: ChannelService instantiates ChannelStore and passes it indirectly to channels via other infrastructure; store lifecycle may need coordination.
related_files.reason_to_check: If changing lifecycle ordering or store construction, validate channel persistence expectations.

related_files.relationship: from_app_config uses get_app_config() to extract channels config from AppConfig.extra; affects what gets passed to ChannelService.
related_files.reason_to_check: If AppConfig shape changes, ensure from_app_config extracts the 'channels' key the same way.

related_files.relationship: resolve_class is used to dynamically import channel classes; changes to resolve_class semantics affect channel loading.
related_files.reason_to_check: If switching to another import/resolution mechanism, ensure error handling and base_class expectations remain aligned.

tests_to_run: pytest -q (run full backend tests if touching lifecycle/manager)

insights.title: Resolve IM channel backend URLs from env with config override
insights.problem: When running channels inside Docker Compose, `localhost` is incorrect; channels needed to use container DNS names.
insights.root_cause: Service used hardcoded localhost fallback and didn't read channel-specific env vars.
insights.solution: Added _resolve_service_url that checks config key, then env var (DEER_FLOW_CHANNELS_LANGGRAPH_URL / DEER_FLOW_CHANNELS_GATEWAY_URL), then default. Tests added to assert env fallback and config override.
insights.lesson_learned: Service components running in containers should resolve backend URLs from environment first and allow config overrides; provide clear env variable names for containerized deployments.

frontend/src/components/workspace/input-.short_description: React InputBox component for the chat workspace that manages model/mode selection, prompt input, and follow-up suggestions.
frontend/src/components/workspace/input-.description:
This file exports the InputBox React component used in the chat workspace. It composes a rich PromptInput UI (attachments, textarea, footer/tools) and centralizes logic for: selecting the active model and input mode, resolving reasoning effort, handling submit/stop actions, pre-filling initial input, and fetching/ showing follow-up suggestions after assistant responses. The component is designed to work with the app's ThreadContext (useThread) and persists thread-level context via onContextChange callbacks. It uses internal state and refs to coordinate confirmation dialogs for suggestion replacement/append, to avoid racing with streaming responses, and to debounce/guard model auto-selection.

The InputBox sits between several subsystems: model management (useModels), thread state/streaming (useThread, as used by the chat page), and the PromptInput "ai-elements" primitives (PromptInputTextarea, PromptInputAttachments, controller hooks). It honors disabled/isMock flags and has careful guards around submitting while a stream is active. It triggers a follow-ups endpoint on the backend after streaming finishes, assembling a small recent conversation window and POSTing to /api/threads/{threadId}/suggestions to get suggested next prompts. The component also handles a modal confirmation flow when a user clicks a follow-up while there is current input (replace vs append). The file integrates with model capabilities (supports_thinking, supports_reasoning_effort) to resolve/limit available modes and reasoning effort options.

Design decisions visible in the code: defaultValue usage for initial input (via initialValue prop), reliance on usePromptInputController to mutate the input programmatically (setInput, value), and optimistic UI behavior when the selected model is not yet flushed to thread settings (guard in handleSubmit that triggers onContextChange then defers actual submit via setTimeout). The file also contains an exported helper getResolvedMode to centralize logic for fallback mode selection when a model does not support 'thinking'. Commit history indicates past refactors to hide raw model IDs in the UI, add suggestions, and expose initialValue; these behavioral invariants are important for consumers and tests.

key_constructs.name: InputBox
key_constructs.purpose: Primary React component that renders the prompt input UI and manages model/mode selection, suggestion fetching, and submit/stop behavior for a chat thread.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the main integration point between the PromptInput primitives, thread state, model capabilities, and follow-up suggestion flow. Edits to this function must preserve the various guards (streaming state, resolvedModelName check, disabled/isMock checks) and the use of the prompt controller to avoid breaking input side-effects.

key_constructs.name: getResolvedMode
key_constructs.purpose: Computes the effective InputMode given a desired mode and whether the model supports 'thinking'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to ensure UI/model state compatibility. Any change here affects defaults and transitions when models lacking 'thinking' capability are selected.

key_behaviors: resolves and updates model_name and input mode on model changes
submits messages via onSubmit and guards against streaming state
fetches follow-up suggestions after an assistant finishes streaming
programmatically sets textarea input via usePromptInputController
manages confirm dialog flow for replacing/appending suggestions

pitfalls.mistake: Treating the textarea as a fully controlled input and replacing defaultValue with controlled value without using the prompt controller.
pitfalls.consequence: Will break programmatic input manipulations (setInput), may cause focus/selection bugs and unexpected re-renders.
pitfalls.prevention: Continue to use usePromptInputController and defaultValue for initialValue; do not convert underlying PromptInput to a normal controlled React input without updating controller usage.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing the resolvedModelName guard in handleSubmit that defers submit until model auto-selection syncs.
pitfalls.consequence: Messages may be sent with an unintended model or mode (race between effect and submit), causing inconsistent behavior between UI and persisted thread settings.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the guard and the setTimeout-based deferred submit behavior, or ensure any replacement maintains the same synchronization semantics.

pitfalls.mistake: Triggering follow-up suggestion fetches in incorrect lifecycle moments (e.g., while streaming or when disabled/isMock is true).
pitfalls.consequence: Unnecessary network requests, confusing UX, or suggestions derived from incomplete thread state.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing checks: only start suggestions flow when streaming transitions from streaming -> not streaming, and when not disabled and not isMock.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the AbortController flow incorrectly or removing cleanup from the suggestion fetch effect.
pitfalls.consequence: Requests can leak or update state after component unmount/thread changes, causing stale suggestions to appear or console errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the controller.abort return cleanup and the .finally/state resets as implemented.

key_sections: Model auto-selection effect (useEffect that sets model_name/mode when models load): ensures thread settings sync with available models.
handleSubmit: guards against streaming and resolves model sync before sending the message.
Follow-up suggestions effect (useEffect triggered when streaming finishes): builds recent window, posts to backend, handles loading and cancellation.
Suggestion confirmation handlers (confirmReplaceAndSend, confirmAppendAndSend): orchestrate replacing/appending input and triggering submit.
skip_unless_needed: Presentational JSX for PromptInput children (attachments/tools/buttons) unless changing UI structure or CSS classes

tests.example_command: No component-level tests provided; to validate build/run use frontend build commands.

related_files.relationship: Provides the surrounding page that consumes InputBox: passes threadId, status, context and onSubmit handlers, and wraps InputBox in ThreadContext. Changes to InputBox should be coordinated with how the page uses onStop/onSubmit and onContextChange.
related_files.reason_to_check: If you change onSubmit/onStop signature, the streaming status semantics, or how initialValue/defaultValue are handled, update the page so thread lifecycle, history.replaceState, and followups padding are consistent.

related_files.relationship: PromptInput primitives and usePromptInputController are imported and relied upon heavily for programmatic input updates and submission.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any API changes to PromptInput or its controller (setInput, value, requestSubmit) require corresponding updates here to preserve behavior.

related_files.relationship: useModels supplies models and capability flags (supports_thinking, supports_reasoning_effort) that InputBox uses to resolve modes and reasoning effort options.
related_files.reason_to_check: If model shape or capability flags change, adjust getResolvedMode, handleModelSelect, and rendering that depends on selectedModel.

tests_to_run: Run the app and exercise the chat page manually: make dev (project root) or make docker-start
data_constants_to_check: Model objects returned from useModels: ensure supports_thinking and supports_reasoning_effort fields exist and have expected types
Backend suggestions endpoint: POST /api/threads/{threadId}/suggestions; ensure contract (request body with messages/n/model_name) remains compatible

insights.title: Hide raw model ID in UI, show display_name only
insights.problem: The input box UI displayed the model internal identifier (selectedModel.model) which may be sensitive or confusing.
insights.root_cause: UI previously rendered both display_name and raw model id in the selector; this exposed internal identifiers to users.
insights.solution: Remove rendering of the internal model id and only display selectedModel.display_name. Also cleaned up formatting changes for readability.
insights.lesson_learned: Prefer showing friendly display names in the UI and avoid exposing internal IDs unless necessary for debugging; adjust tests if they relied on the raw ID.

insights.title: Add suggestions UI and integrate prompt input controller
insights.problem: Users needed quick starter prompts; input state updates and focusing behavior were inconsistent across flows.
insights.root_cause: No centralized suggestion widget existed and input initialization/focusing was handled ad-hoc.
insights.solution: Introduce SuggestionList and Suggestion components, use usePromptInputController to programmatically set the input value and selection ranges, and add logic to hide suggestions when mode=skill (via useSearchParams). Also support initialValue via defaultValue prop for the PromptInput textarea.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding interactive suggestion features that manipulate the input value, use the provided input controller / provider to avoid direct DOM-only approaches; feature gating by URL mode should use useSearchParams consistently.

insights.title: Expose initialValue prop for InputBox
insights.problem: There needed to be a way to prefill the input for flows like 'create skill'.
insights.root_cause: Input prefilling was previously handled only by DOM manipulation; not via component props.
insights.solution: Add initialValue?: string prop and pass it as defaultValue to the underlying PromptInput textarea.
insights.lesson_learned: Prefer declarative initialValue/defaultValue props on input components for predictable initialization (and still use controller when centralizing input state).

docker/provisioner/app.py.short_description: A FastAPI-based service that provisions per-sandbox Kubernetes Pods and NodePort Services for DeerFlow sandboxes.
docker/provisioner/app.py.description:
This module implements the DeerFlow Sandbox Provisioner: an HTTP service (FastAPI) that dynamically creates and manages a single Pod + NodePort Service per sandbox_id in a target Kubernetes cluster. It exists so the backend can request an isolated sandbox instance (containerized sandbox server image) that will be reachable at a host/node address (NODE_HOST:NodePort). The provisioner mounts the host kubeconfig (or uses in-cluster config), ensures the target namespace exists, and creates K8s V1Pod and V1Service resources with appropriate hostPath volumes to expose skills and thread-specific user-data directories.

Design choices visible in the file: (1) runtime configuration is read exclusively from environment variables (K8S_NAMESPACE, SANDBOX_IMAGE, SKILLS_HOST_PATH, THREADS_HOST_PATH, KUBECONFIG_PATH, NODE_HOST, K8S_API_SERVER) so the service is portable in Docker and in-cluster deployments; (2) validation and filesystem path handling were implemented explicitly to preserve Windows path semantics (join_host_path) and to whitelist thread identifiers (_validate_thread_id) used to construct hostPath mount points; (3) initialization is performed in a FastAPI lifespan context manager to load kubeconfig / in-cluster config and create the namespace before serving requests; (4) operations are defensive: create endpoints attempt idempotency (check for existing NodePort), attempt small polling for NodePort allocation, and roll back created resources if subsequent creation fails. The file also suppresses InsecureRequestWarning when connecting to clusters with self-signed certs and supports rewriting the kube API server via K8S_API_SERVER for Docker->host connections.

This file integrates with the rest of DeerFlow by providing reachable sandbox instances for the backend's sandbox middleware/providers. The Pod manifests mount /mnt/skills (read-only) and per-thread hostPath directories under THREADS_HOST_PATH to share user-data between host and Pod. The provisioner therefore directly interacts with the host filesystem semantics and the Kubernetes API, so changes must respect path formats, security constraints, and the API lifecycle semantics used here. Note: the file shown is truncated at the end in the provided snapshot — additional endpoints and listing/GET behavior are likely present later in the file and should be reviewed before editing.

key_constructs.name: join_host_path
key_constructs.purpose: Join host filesystem path segments while preserving native Windows or POSIX path styles.
key_constructs.reasoning: Constructs hostPath volume paths for Pod manifests in a cross-platform-safe way; prevents corrupting Windows drive-letter paths and is used when creating hostPath paths for per-thread user-data directories.

key_constructs.name: _validate_thread_id
key_constructs.purpose: Validate thread_id against a SAFE_THREAD_ID_PATTERN to ensure only safe characters are used.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents unsafe characters in thread IDs that would be embedded into hostPath paths and Kubernetes resource labels/names; callers rely on this to avoid path injection or invalid label names.

key_constructs.name: KUBECONFIG_PATH
key_constructs.purpose: Environment-configurable path to the kubeconfig file as seen inside the provisioner container.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by initialization logic to load an external kubeconfig; edits must preserve semantics because missing or incorrect values change how the provisioner attempts to connect to Kubernetes (file vs in-cluster).

key_constructs.name: _init_k8s_client
key_constructs.purpose: Initialize and return a kubernetes CoreV1Api client, attempting kubeconfig first then in-cluster, with optional API server override.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates connection strategy and SSL verification handling (when K8S_API_SERVER is set, verify_ssl is disabled). Be cautious when changing behavior that affects certificate verification or host rewriting.

key_constructs.name: _wait_for_kubeconfig
key_constructs.purpose: Block/poll until a kubeconfig file appears at KUBECONFIG_PATH (up to a timeout), with validation of file vs directory.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to delay startup until a mounted kubeconfig is available in Docker scenarios; changing timing or error handling affects service readiness and failure modes.

key_constructs.name: _ensure_namespace
key_constructs.purpose: Ensure the target Kubernetes namespace exists, creating it if necessary.
key_constructs.reasoning: Idempotently creates the configured K8S_NAMESPACE with labels; relies on core_v1 global client and handles 404 via ApiException.

key_constructs.name: lifespan
key_constructs.purpose: FastAPI lifespan context manager that initializes the Kubernetes client and ensures the namespace on app startup.
key_constructs.reasoning: Main initialization entry point for the FastAPI app; changes here alter what happens before the app starts serving requests.

key_constructs.name: CreateSandboxRequest
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic model for incoming sandbox creation requests, validating sandbox_id and thread_id.
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines the request contract for create_sandbox endpoint; the thread_id field uses a regex pattern to enforce SAFE_THREAD_ID_PATTERN.

key_constructs.name: SandboxResponse
key_constructs.purpose: Pydantic response model returned when creating or querying a sandbox, containing id, URL, and status.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures uniform response shape for the API consumers (backend) that need the direct sandbox URL and Pod status.

key_constructs.name: _build_pod
key_constructs.purpose: Construct a kubernetes.client.V1Pod manifest configured for the sandbox image, probes, resources, volumes, and mounts.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encodes the Pod spec (container image, ports, probes, resources, hostPath mounts for skills and user-data) used when creating the Pod; any change here changes runtime resource usage, mount locations, readiness checks, and security context.

key_constructs.name: _build_service
key_constructs.purpose: Construct a NodePort V1Service manifest that selects the sandbox Pod and exposes port 8080.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to create a NodePort service so the backend can access the sandbox by mapping nodePort → container port; leaving nodePort unspecified relies on K8s dynamic allocation and the subsequent polling logic to find the chosen port.

key_constructs.name: _get_node_port
key_constructs.purpose: Read and return the NodePort assigned to the sandbox Service (if present).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to implement idempotency in create_sandbox and to determine the sandbox_url; returns None if the service or port is not found or an ApiException occurs.

key_constructs.name: _get_pod_phase
key_constructs.purpose: Return the phase (Pending/Running/etc.) of the sandbox Pod, or 'NotFound' on failure.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to populate the status field in SandboxResponse and provide basic health/state reporting for the occupying Pod.

key_constructs.name: create_sandbox
key_constructs.purpose: FastAPI endpoint that creates (idempotently) a Pod + NodePort Service for a given sandbox_id/thread_id and returns its URL and status.
key_constructs.reasoning: High-level orchestration: checks for existing allocation, creates Pod and Service if needed, polls for nodePort allocation, and returns status; handles ApiException rollbacks for partial failures.

key_constructs.name: destroy_sandbox
key_constructs.purpose: FastAPI endpoint that deletes the sandbox Service and Pod for a given sandbox_id, returning success or a partial-cleanup error.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements cleanup logic with error aggregation; ensures 404s are ignored while other ApiException errors are surfaced as HTTP 500 partial cleanup errors.

key_behaviors: creates a Kubernetes Pod and NodePort Service per sandbox_id
returns a reachable sandbox URL (http://{NODE_HOST}:{NodePort})
idempotently detects existing sandboxes and returns existing nodePort
destroys sandbox Pod and Service and reports partial cleanup errors
initializes Kubernetes client using mounted kubeconfig or in-cluster config

pitfalls.mistake: Assume KUBECONFIG_PATH always points to a regular file and start without checking.
pitfalls.consequence: If KUBECONFIG_PATH is a directory or missing, startup will raise an error or the provisioner will incorrectly attempt in-cluster config. This can lead to failure to contact the host cluster.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing _wait_for_kubeconfig/_init_k8s_client logic that validates file vs directory before using it.

pitfalls.mistake: Modify hostPath join logic without preserving Windows path semantics.
pitfalls.consequence: HostPath values written into Pod manifests could become invalid on Windows hosts (drive-letter lost or backslashes converted), breaking mounts and causing Pod failures.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep join_host_path behavior intact when constructing PATHs for hostPath volumes; any change must continue to support both POSIX and Windows formats.

pitfalls.mistake: Change SAFE_THREAD_ID_PATTERN or remove _validate_thread_id without reviewing callers.
pitfalls.consequence: Invalid characters could end up in hostPath directories, Kubernetes labels, or Pod names, leading to mount/path injection vulnerabilities or K8s resource creation failures.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure thread IDs remain validated before being embedded into filesystem paths and resource names.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume NodePort is immediately available after Service creation.
pitfalls.consequence: The code polls up to a short loop for node port allocation; reducing or removing this loop can result in returning missing URLs or race conditions with backend consumers.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the polling/wait approach or adjust timeout values carefully if changing NodePort allocation handling.

pitfalls.mistake: Change _init_k8s_client behavior that disables SSL verification when K8S_API_SERVER is set.
pitfalls.consequence: Clients connecting to clusters with self-signed certs may suddenly fail or become insecure; changing this without considering the environment can break Docker-to-host K8s setups.
pitfalls.prevention: When modifying the K8S_API_SERVER handling, ensure compatibility with self-signed certs and document verification implications.

key_sections: CreateSandboxRequest: defines the contract for create sandbox calls
_build_pod: important to understand volumes, mounts, probes, and resource requests/limits
create_sandbox: orchestrates Pod+Service creation, idempotency checks, polling for NodePort, and rollback behavior
_init_k8s_client: shows kubeconfig vs in-cluster logic and K8S_API_SERVER override
skip_unless_needed: logging.basicConfig setup
urllib3 InsecureRequestWarning suppression
trivial helper functions such as _pod_name/_svc_name

tests.example_command: cd docker/provisioner && uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8002  # run service locally (ensure KUBECONFIG_PATH is mounted)

related_files.relationship: The provisioner creates hostPath mounts for directories that LocalSandboxProvider expects on the host (threads/user-data and skills).
related_files.reason_to_check: If mount paths or THREADS_HOST_PATH/SKILLS_HOST_PATH semantics change here, the sandbox provider must be updated to match the host-side directory layout.

related_files.relationship: The backend sandbox middleware consumes sandbox URLs provided by the provisioner to connect to ephemeral sandboxes.
related_files.reason_to_check: If the sandbox URL format or port allocation strategy changes, middleware and clients that call the provisioner must be updated.

related_files.relationship: Development compose wiring references NODE_HOST, kubeconfig mounts and port routing that affects how the provisioner is used.
related_files.reason_to_check: When changing environment variables (NODE_HOST, KUBECONFIG_PATH) or service ports, update compose files to keep runtime wiring correct.

tests_to_run: run provisioner locally: KUBECONFIG_PATH must be mounted and reachable; start with uvicorn docker.provisioner.app:app --port 8002

insights.title: Windows-preserving join and thread_id validation for provisioner
insights.problem: Provisioner constructed pod hostPath volumes using simple string concatenation that could break on Windows-style paths and lacked safe thread_id validation.
insights.root_cause: Paths were constructed without preserving Windows drive syntax and no validation function was shared.
insights.solution: Added join_host_path to preserve Windows path style and _validate_thread_id with SAFE_THREAD_ID_PATTERN; used join_host_path for hostPath volume paths and validated thread_id before building K8s Pod manifest.
insights.lesson_learned: Infrastructure code that produces host filesystem paths must preserve native path formats and validate identifiers used in paths.

insights.title: Add find-skills public skill
insights.problem: No skill existed for discovering/installing other skills.
insights.root_cause: Ecosystem benefit: users need a discoverability assistant.
insights.solution: Add find-skills SKILL.md documentation and install script to guide npx skills usage and link installation flow.
insights.lesson_learned: Providing meta-skills (skills that help manage other skills) improves discoverability and onboarding for extensible agent platforms.

insights.title: Install-skill helper script
insights.problem: Users need a simple CLI to install public skills into local project.
insights.root_cause: Manual npx skills add and file linking were error-prone.
insights.solution: Create install-skill.sh that finds project root (deer-flow.code-workspace), runs npx skills add -g, and links installed skill into skills/custom.
insights.lesson_learned: Helper scripts should verify inputs and project root and be idempotent; consider permission issues and cross-platform behavior (symlinks on Windows).

insights.title: Add github-deep-research skill documentation
insights.problem: No prebuilt skill for deep repository research.
insights.root_cause: Users asked for an automated research workflow.
insights.solution: Add comprehensive SKILL.md describing multi-round GitHub-centric research workflow and output template.
insights.lesson_learned: Complex skills need clear workflow docs and templates; include explicit command examples for reproducibility.

insights.title: Add and adjust report template formatting
insights.problem: Report template needed consistent formatting and a language note.
insights.root_cause: Template lacked an instruction to generate report in user's language and consistent bullet formatting.
insights.solution: Add '[!NOTE]' language instruction and normalize markdown bullet formatting/spacing.
insights.lesson_learned: Report templates should be locale-aware and consistent; include metadata placeholders and usage instructions.
